coredata.dc:title: Dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescence sensing system for the detection of captopril based on Fe/NC nanozymes and carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Maolin
Lv, Yuntai
Lin, Zihan
coredata.dc:description: Metal nitrogen-doped carbon (MNC) nanozymes have received increasing attention in bio-catalysis filed due to adequate catalytic activity, outstanding stability and reusability. Herein, the Fe/NC nanozymes (Fe/NC NZs) with peroxidase-like activity was successfully synthesized and a fluorescence turn on and colorimetric dual-mode sensing system was developed for quantification of captopril (CP) based on Fe/NC NZs and orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs). The Fe/NC NZs as an enzyme mimic can efficiently catalyze the 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) chromogenic reaction, forming blue-colored oxidized TMB product (oxTMB) with the presence of H2O2, leading to the fluorescence quenching of O-CDs simultaneously via the inner filter effect (IFE). When CP was present, the blue oxTMB was reduced to colorless TMB, resulting in the inhibition of IFE and the recovery of fluorescence of O-CDs. The fluorescence increase of O-CDs and absorbance decrease of oxTMB depended on CP concentration. Good linear relationships of fluorescence and colorimetric sensing towards CP were obtained in the range from 1 to 50 μM, and the detection limits were 0.47 and 0.56 μM, respectively. Moreover, this as-constructed dual-mode sensor was used to detect CP in pharmaceutical products with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe/NC nanozymes
Dual-mode
Fluorescence
Colorimetric
Carbon dots
Captopril

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Colorimetric and fluorometric assay of CP
Assay of CP in pharmaceutical samples
Characterization of Fe/NC NZs and O-CDs
Study on peroxidase-like activity, mechanism and kinetics of Fe/NC NZs
The detection mechanism and feasibility for CP

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis process of Fe/NC NZs. (B) TEM image of Fe/NC NZs. Scale bar: 200 nm. (C) XRD patterns of various samples. (D) XPS survey spectra of the Fe/NC NZs. (E) XPS spectra for N 1 s regions of Fe/NC NZs.
(A) TEM image of O-CDs. The insert: HRTEM images of O-CDs. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum (dotted line) and fluorescence emission spectra of O-CDs excited at different wavelengths.

(A) The effects of O-CDs, H2O2 and Fe/NC NZs on TMB at 45℃ for 15 min. (B) Fluorescence probe tests: 0.2 mM TA, H2O2 + 0.2 mM TA and Fe/NC NZs + H2O2 + TA with various concentrations. (C) Changes the absorbance in the presence of different concentrations of IPA. (D) Schematic diagram of the mechanism of the peroxidase-mimic activity of Fe/NC NZs.

(A) Fluorescence emission of O-CDs and UV − vis spectrum of oxTMB. (B) The zeta potentials of O-CDs and oxTMB. (C) Fluorescence decay curves of O-CDs with or without oxTMB. (D) UV − vis spectrum of oxTMB, O-CDs and O-CDs/oxTMB. Fluorescence emission spectra (E) and UV–vis absorption spectra (F) of Fe/NC NZs/H2O2/TMB/O-CDs system with various substances. Inset: photographs of the corresponding analytical solution.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Fe/NC NZs/H2O2/TMB/O-CDs system after the introduction of different contents of CP. (B) The regression fit of F/F0 versus the concentration of CP. (C) Absorption spectra of the Fe/NC NZs/H2O2/TMB/O-CDs system in the presence of CP with varied concentrations. (D) Concentration-response curve for CP determination.

Selectivity of the dual-mode sensor for fluorescence (A) and colorimetric (C) detection of CP. anti-interference ability of dual-mode sensor for CP fluorescence (B) and colorimetric (D) assay towards the coexisting interference substances. Concentration: 50 μM CP; 500 μM ions; 5 μM d-penicillamine, 6-mercaptopurine, cysteine, glutathione, uric acid and ascorbic acid; 250 μM other interfering substances.
Schematic diagram of the fluorescence and colorimetric-based detection platforms.
Results of CP detection in pharmaceutical samples.

head.ce:title: Dual-mode colorimetric and fluorescence sensing system for the detection of captopril based on Fe/NC nanozymes and carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun 130022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A high sensitive and selective dual-mode sensor for colorimetric and fluorescent detection of CP in pharmaceutical formulations.

Metal nitrogen-doped carbon (MNC) nanozymes have received increasing attention in bio-catalysis filed due to adequate catalytic activity, outstanding stability and reusability. Herein, the Fe/NC nanozymes (Fe/NC NZs) with peroxidase-like activity was successfully synthesized and a fluorescence turn on and colorimetric dual-mode sensing system was developed for quantification of captopril (CP) based on Fe/NC NZs and orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs). The Fe/NC NZs as an enzyme mimic can efficiently catalyze the 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) chromogenic reaction, forming blue-colored oxidized TMB product (oxTMB) with the presence of H2O2, leading to the fluorescence quenching of O-CDs simultaneously via the inner filter effect (IFE). When CP was present, the blue oxTMB was reduced to colorless TMB, resulting in the inhibition of IFE and the recovery of fluorescence of O-CDs. The fluorescence increase of O-CDs and absorbance decrease of oxTMB depended on CP concentration. Good linear relationships of fluorescence and colorimetric sensing towards CP were obtained in the range from 1 to 50 μM, and the detection limits were 0.47 and 0.56 μM, respectively. Moreover, this as-constructed dual-mode sensor was used to detect CP in pharmaceutical products with satisfactory results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past 10 years, nanozymes have attracted extensive attention as potential alternatives to natural enzymes. In contrast to natural enzymes, nanozymes possess splendid advantages, such as flexible structure design, cost effectiveness, tunable activity and higher stability and robustness [1]. Usually, nanozymes are classified as noble metal nanomaterials, metallic oxide nanoparticles and carbon-based nanomaterials [2,3]. Among of them, the carbon-based nanomaterials have advantage of high stability, good biocompatibility, low cost and obtained more attraction. However, most of currently developed carbon-based nanozymes showed poor catalytic efficiency. Thus, it is of great interest to develop novel artificial enzymes with high activity.

Recently, metal nitrogen-doped carbon (MNC) nanostructures have gained plentiful attention because of the remarkable activity and diverse synthetic approaches. Particularly, the MNC nanomaterials have prominent enzyme-like activity, due to the similar structure with some natural enzymes with special metal active centers [4]. Furthermore, doping metals into the N-doped carbon can not only create active sites but also improve their electrical conductivity. MNC nanozymes catalyst with peroxidase properties can catalyze hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), it is widely used in biosensing area. For example, Zuo et al. developed a sensor array based on CoNC nanozymes for colorimetric discrimination of antioxidants (dopamine, glutathione, cysteine, ascorbic acid et al.) [5]. Chen et al. utilized FeNC nanozymes with peroxidase-mimicking activity for accurate analysis of ascorbic acid-related bioenzymes [6]. Huang et al. used the CuNC nanozymes for the monitoring of ascorbic acid, H2O2 and glucose. Moreover, The CuNC nanozymes was successfully utilized for the determination of total antioxidant capacity in real drinks [7]. Until now, there has been no reported about utilizing the enzyme-mimicking properties of MNC nanozymes for captopril assays.

Captopril (CP), chemically designated as 1-[(2S)-3-mercapto-2-methylpropionyl]-l-proline, as an active inhibitor of enzyme, is used to treat coronary heart disease, hypertension and angina pectoris [8,9]. Despite its remarkable efficacy, CP can cause some unfavorable side effects like kidney failure, anorexia, chronic dry cough, fatigue and fainting [10]. Moreover, the overdose of CP can lead to suppression of bone marrow and proteinuria [11,12]. Thus, it is of considerable importance to develop reliable methods to quality control the pharmaceutical formulations and monitor patients. Presently, the methods for quantitative determining CP include spectrophotometry [13,14], electrochemistry [15–17], mass spectrometry [18], capillary electrophoresis [19], chemiluminescence [20] and HPLC [21]. However, certain drawbacks limited the application of the mentioned methods such as complicated procedure, professional skills and the need for expensive instruments. Therefore, a sensitive, quick and simple method for CP analysis is demanded.

Some fluorescence and colorimetric methods have been reported to quantitatively detect CP. With these approaches, He et al. established a “turn on” fluorescent probe to detect CP in biological samples [11]. Deng et al. reported a reliable, simple and environmentally benign method for monitoring CP using the fluorescence quenching effect of MnO4 -on B and N codoped carbon quantum dots [22]. Zhang et al. demonstrated a new strategy to achieve reversible regulation of peroxidase-like activity of MoS2 quantum dots, which was used to establish the colorimetry for CP [23]. As expected, CP could be sensitively monitored by fluorescence method, and could be conveniently monitored through colorimetric method. If combining fluorescence and colorimetric assays together, it will provide a more sensitive, effective and convenient method for the assay of CP. Therefore, developing a sensitive, credible, rapid and visual dual-mode method for the detection of CP is highly desirable.

Compared with other fluorescent materials, carbon dots (CDs) possess unique properties, including water dispersibility, low cytotoxicity, biological compatibility, photostability and so on [24]. Thus, employing CDs to design novel probes may be a promising proposal for biosensing. So far, many CDs-based nanoprobes have been reported for the detection of biological molecules. For example, Liao et al. developed a highly selective and sensitive pyrophosphate ion (PPi) detection approach through the use of nanozymatic CDs [25]. Lu et al. prepared Mo, S co-doped carbon dots (Mo-CDs) with high peroxidase mimetic catalytic activity, which were used to construct a cascade colorimetric biosensor to detect cholesterol [26].

In this study, the peroxidase-like activity of Fe/NC nanozymes (Fe/NC NZs) was successfully demonstrated. A colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode based on the inner-filter effect (IFE) between orange-emitting carbon dots (O-CDs) and oxidized 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (oxTMB) for the sensitive quantification of CP was successfully established. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , Fe/NC NZs can enable the decomposition of H2O2 to hydroxyl radical, the generated hydroxyl radical further oxidize TMB to generate oxTMB, causing the solution to change color from colorless to blue. The absorption of the produced oxTMB overlapped with the emission fluorescence of the O-CDs, the IFE induced fluorescence quenching of O-CDs can occur efficiently. However, the blue oxTMB can be reduced to colorless TMB in the presence of CP. As the result, the efficient fluorescence recovery of the quenched O-CDs can be observed due to the inhibition of IFE between oxTMB and O-CDs. Based on that, dual-mode “on–off” colorimetric and “off–on” fluorescence detection of CP is constructed. In addition, as far as we know, there is no colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode sensor to be developed for detection CP simultaneously by now.
Reagents and apparatus were listed in the Electronic Supplementary Material.

The preparation of Fe/NC NZs was accorded to previously reported method [27]. As shown in Fig. 1 A, typically, 1314 mg of 2-methylimidazole was dissolved in 15 mL of methanol as solution A. In another methanol solution (30 mL), 846 mg of Fe(acac)3 and 1190 mg of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O were mixed under ultrasound for 20 min to get a uniform solution B. Subsequently, two solutions were mixed, followed by vigorous stirring at ambient temperature for 1 h. Then, the mixed solution was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave (100 mL) and heated for 4 h at 120 ℃. After cooling, the obtained Fe(acac)3@ZIF-8 was centrifuged and rinsed with DMF and methanol for several times, respectively. The collected samples were dried at 70 ℃ under vacuum overnight. The powder of as-synthesized Fe(acac)3@ZIF-8 was ground and transferred to a tube furnace. Finally, the powder was heated to 800℃ with a heating rate of 3℃/min under N2 flow and held at that temperature for 3 h and then naturally cooled to room temperature in N2 atmosphere. The obtained materials were immersed in 1 M HNO3 for 24 h under continuous stirring to remove Fe nanoparticles, FeOx, and other inactive Fe species. Finally, Fe/NC NZs was obtained after washing with deionized water and drying.

O-CDs were prepared by a solvothermal method according to previous report with certain modifications [28]. In a typical synthesis route, Rhodamine B (20 mg) was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water under ultra-sonication. The solution was transferred into a 15 mL polytetrafluoroethylene autoclave, and heated at 160 ℃ for 2 h. The resultant O-CDs solution was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 15 min) to collect the supernatant. Then this supernatant was dialyzed for 12 h in a dialysis bag. Finally, the O-CDs solution was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorometric assay of CP
ce:section.ce:para: The CP detection is described in detail as follows:30 μL of H2O2 solution (10 mM), 20 μL 10 mM TMB and 400 μL NaAc-HAc buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.0) were mixed with ultrapure water. Next, 20 μL 100 μg/mL Fe/NC NZs was introduced into the above solution, followed by incubation at 45℃ for 15 min. Further, different amounts of CP (5 mM) were added into the above mixture solution. The solution was allowed to stand for 4 min, and 20 μL O-CDs was injected to the mixed solution (the total volume of the solution was 1 mL). Thereafter, the absorbance spectra and fluorescence spectra of the solutions were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of CP in pharmaceutical samples
ce:section.ce:para: Captopril tablets obtained from a local pharmacy were produced by Shanghai Pu Kang Pharmaceutical Co. (Shanghai, China). In detail, ten captopril tablets were firstly completely ground into powers by using a mortar and pestle. Then the powders (25 mg) were accurately weighted and dissolved in deionized water (100 mL) via 20 mins ultrasonication. This sample was filtered with 0.22 μM membrane filters. The obtained sample solutions with appropriate dilution were taken for assay of CP based on the procedure of Section 2.4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Fe/NC NZs and O-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis scheme for Fe/NC NZs is exhibited in Fig. 1A, Fe/NC NZs were prepared through an “isolation-pyrolysis” method. Briefly, Fe(acac)3 molecular were trapped in the cavities of ZIF-8 by a hydrothermal method to prepared the Fe(acac)3@ZIF-8. Fe/NC NZs was finally obtained by pyrolysis of Fe(acac)3@ZIF-8 under N2 atmosphere. The morphologies of Fe/NC NZs were verified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The TEM images in Fig. 1B reveal that Fe/NC NZs had a well-defined dodecahedron structure with an average particle size of around 150 nm. The crystal structure of Fe/NC NZs was investigated using XRD (Fig. 1C). Compared with ZIF-8 standard pattern, the diffraction peak positions of Fe(acac)3@ZIF-8 shifted slightly, suggesting the successful encapsulation of the precursor molecules. After pyrolysis, The XRD pattern only showed two broad diffraction peaks in the range of 20-30◦ and 40-50◦, which were assigned to the (002) and (100) planes of graphitic carbon, respectively [29–31]. Meanwhile, no characteristic peaks for Fe or its carbides/oxides were detected, which suggested that iron may be dispersed on the carbon matrix. To reveal the chemical composition, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed. The elements of N, C, Fe and O in the Fe/NC NZs could be observed in the XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 1D). The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 1E) revealed the coexistence of pyridinic-N (398.7 eV), pyrrolic-N (399.9 eV) and graphitic-N (400.8 eV) [27,32]. The pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N are regarded to serve as anchor points for iron atoms [33–35].

The synthesized O-CDs were measured by TEM. The TEM image in Fig. 2 A showed that O-CDs were evenly distributed and nearly spherical in shape, and the average diameter is 2.5 nm. The HRTEM images (inset top right) clearly exhibited the crystalline characteristics of O-CDs with lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm. Fluorescence emission and UV–vis absorption spectra were performed to analyze the optical properties of O-CDs. In the UV–vis spectra of O-CDs, an absorption peak at 554 nm could be observed. Under different excitation wavelength (520–550 nm), the O-CDs displayed a typical excitation-independent emission behavior (Fig. 2B).

The photostability tests were conducted to verify the feasibility of O-CDs in sensing applications. Firstly, the fluorescence (FL) intensity of O-CDs was almost stable from low salt concentration to high salt concentration solutions (Fig. S1A). Then, the fluorescence stability of O-CDs in different pH solutions were investigated. The results revealed that the FL intensity of O-CDs maintain stable in pH range of pH 3.2–7.2 (Fig. S1B), manifesting that the O-CDs had good fluorescence stability in acidic and neutral solutions. In addition, the FL intensity of the O-CDs stored at room temperature remained almost unchanged for 14 days (Fig. S1C). Fig. S1D displayed that the FL intensity of the O-CDs was only reduced by 13 % after exposed for 1 h under the xenon lamp radiation. Taking the results above, the synthesized fluorescent O-CDs possess excellent photostability and have the potential to be used as an ideal fluorescence probe for biological sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on peroxidase-like activity, mechanism and kinetics of Fe/NC NZs
ce:section.ce:para: The peroxidase mimicking activity of Fe/NC NZs was researched through its catalytic oxidation of typical chromogenic substrates by H2O2, where N,N- diethyl-p-phenylenediamine (DPD), 2,2′-azino-bis(3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) (ABTS), TMB and 1,2-diaminobenzene (OPD) were used as substrates. As depicted in Fig. S2, under the presence of Fe/NC NZs and H2O2, the oxidative products of the four chromogenic substrates showed pink, green, blue and yellow color with characteristic absorption peaks at 551 nm, 416 nm, 652 nm and 448 nm, respectively. These results indicated the intrinsic peroxidase mimicking activity of Fe/NC NZs. In our current study, TMB was chosen as substrate for further experiments. The oxidation product of TMB could quench the fluorescence of O-CDs effectively, further used for constructing a sensitive and accurate colorimetric and fluorescence dual-mode sensing system.

To find out whether the catalytic activity of the sensing system depends on the Fe/NC NZs, the ultraviolet absorptions were tested. Based on the spectrum in Fig. 3 A, TMB transferred into oxTMB with catalytic by Fe/NC NZs only when H2O2 was introduced in reaction system. To study the mechanism of Fe/NC NZs peroxidase mimics catalyzing H2O2 decomposition, we conducted the fluorescent probe and scavenger addition experiments. Terephthalic Acid (TA) was employed as a fluorescence probe to confirm the existence of ·OH [36,37]. In Fig. 3B, After co-incubation with H2O2 and the Fe/NC NZs, the non-fluorescent TA translated into its fluorescent product with a strong blue florescence due to the reaction with ·OH radicals. The catalytic mechanism of Fe/NC NZs was further excavated by introducing isopropanol as the scavenger of ·OH radicals [38]. Evident from Fig. 3C, the absorbance of oxTMB noticeably decreased after the addition of isopropanol. Therefore, Fe/NC NZs activated H2O2 to generate ·OH, which catalyzes the oxidation of TMB. Furthermore, steady-state kinetics was studied to investigate the catalytic of Fe/NC NZs. By altering the concentration of the substrates (H2O2, TMB), typical Michaelis-Menten curves were acquired (Fig. S3). The two important parameters of the maximal reaction velocity (Vmax) and Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) calculated by the Lineweaver-Burk plots. Table S1 presented that the Km values of Fe/NC NZs toward TMB (0.18 mM) and H2O2 (0.06 mM). And they were much lower than that of HRP, indicating the Fe/NC NZs had a stronger affinity to TMB and H2O2. Compared with most peroxidase mimics, Km of Fe/NC NZs to H2O2 or TMB is smaller than most of them, indicating that the material possess a considerable peroxidase-like activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The detection mechanism and feasibility for CP
ce:section.ce:para: To verify the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of the O-CDs by oxTMB, the fluorescence emission spectra and UV–vis absorption were conducted. As showed in Fig. 4 A, the absorbance band of the oxTMB had a good overlap with emission spectra of O-CDs, which implied high possibility for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or IFE between O-CDs and oxTMB [39]. The zeta potential was also measured. As showed in Fig. 4B, the zeta potentials of O-CDs and oxTMB were + 3.98 mV and + 10.55 mV, respectively. Thus, oxTMB cannot be absorbed on the surface of O-CDs through electrostatic interactions. This phenomenon revealed that the FRET cannot occur in this system [40,41]. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime was tested to verify the quenching mechanism. Fig. 4C displayed that the fluorescence lifetime of O-CDs/oxTMB remains unchanged compared to O-CDs, demonstrating the IFE based quenching mechanism. Furthermore, oxTMB had no significant absorption spectral change with the addition of O-CDs, which revealed no interaction or complex formation between O-CDs and oxTMB and ruling out static quenching (Fig. 4D) [42].

On the basis of the above finding on the IFE process between oxTMB and O-CDs, the Fe/NC NZs/H2O2/TMB/O-CDs sensing system can be used for the colorimetric and fluorescent dual-mode determination of CP. Given the above discussions, Fe/NC NZs with the peroxidase-like property can oxidize TMB into oxTMB in the presence of H2O2, with strong absorption peak at 652 nm. And the fluorescence of O-CDs at 588 nm will be quenched through IFE process. As visible in Fig. 4E, TMB, Fe/NC NZs, CP or H2O2 did not affect the emission intensity of O-CDs. the fluorescence of O-CDs at 588 nm was obviously reduced with the introduction of Fe/NC NZs/H2O2/TMB. However, the addition of CP into the Fe/NC NZs/H2O2/TMB/O-CDs system resulted in the recovery of fluorescence of O-CDs due to the reduction of oxTMB to TMB by CP. In addition, the feasibility of the sensing system for colorimetric determination of CP was also investigated (Fig. 4F). Fe/NC NZs catalyzed the chromogenic reaction of TMB to form its oxidized product (oxTMB) with strong absorbance at 652 nm in the presence of H2O2. However, the addition of CP reduced the blue oxTMB to colorless TMB, and the absorption intensity of 652 nm decreased. Therefore, the proposed method for fluorescence/colorimetric dual-mode sensing of CP is feasible.

To further optimize the enzyme activities of Fe/NC NZs, the effects of pH and temperature on the enzymatic activity of Fe/NC NZs were explored. As depicted in Fig. S4A, Fe/NC NZs exhibited the best catalytic activity at pH 4.0. As shown in Fig. S4B, The peroxidase-like activity of Fe/NC NZs increased initially and then decreased and reached the highest activity at 45 °C. Thus, 45 ℃ was chosen as the reaction temperature. Furthermore, the TMB concentration and reaction time for constructing Fe/NC NZs/H2O2/TMB/O-CDs system were optimized. Fig. S4C displayed that the FL intensity of O-CDs was quenched with the increased of the TMB concentration. The FL intensity of O-CDs reached a stable level at the TMB of 200 μM, so 0.2 mM was used in the subsequent experiments. As depicted in Fig. S4D, after the introduction of O-CDs, the FL intensity decreased markedly in 30 s and reached a stable level after 1 min, thus, 1 min was used in further experiments.

Under optimized conditions, the performance of the current strategy for the quantitative determination of CP was investigated. As observed in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescent signal of Fe/NC NZs/H2O2/TMB/O-CDs system increased with the concentrations of CP increasing, and the fluorescence intensity increased linearly in the range from 1 to 50 μM. As a result, the linear relationship can be described as F/F0 = 1.195 + 0.019[CP] (R2 = 0.993) (F0 and F are the emission intensity of O-CDs before and after the addition of CP), and the LOD of CP was estimated to be 0.47 μM (based on 3σ/slope rule). In contrast, following the increment concentrations of CP, the absorption signal at 652 nm gradually decreased. After being treated with different concentrations of CP (0–50 μM), a concentration-dependent absorption intensity of the Fe/NC NZs/H2O2/TMB/O-CDs sensing system was obtained (Fig. 5C). In the CP range of 1–50 μM, a linear calibration for CP detection was obtained and fitted as ΔA = 0.0735 + 0.0175[CP] (R2 = 0.995), (ΔA = Ablank-ACP). The LOD can reach 0.56 μM based on 3σ/slope rule. A comparison between the previous reported analysis method for CP and the as-constructed dual-mode sensing strategy was given in Table S2, this method has a suitable detection limit and wider linear range. In addition, compared to single-mode CP assays, dual-mode detection is expected to improve the reliability and accuracy of assay.

The selectivity is an important index to assess the sensor’s analytical performance. To explore the selectivity of the developed assay towards CP, the absorbance values and the fluorescence responses of sensing platform for various substances were tested. The investigated substances were saccharides (glucose, sucrose), inorganic ions (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Cl-, NO3 –), amino acids (histidine, alanine, threonine, phenylalanine, arginine), drugs (paracetamol, artemisinin). As seen in Fig. 6 A and 6C, the addition of these interfering substances separately without CP did not exhibit a significant change of fluorescence and absorbance intensity. Upon the addition of CP, the fluorescence and absorbance signal increased significantly. It was worth noting that the addition of cysteine, glutathione, uric acid, ascorbic acid, d-penicillamine and 6-mercaptopurine had no significance effect on the sensing platform. As shown in Fig. 6B and 6D, the fluorescence and absorbance signal of the detection systems were approximately equal to that of pure CP, when CP were coexisted with these substances. These results clearly demonstrated the highly selectivity and strong capability to resist interferences from coexisting substances for CP sensing.

To prove the feasibility of dual-mode sensing method, pharmaceutical samples were tested for the detection of CP. Table 1 listed the results for analysis of CP in pharmaceutical samples using fluorescent and colorimetric assay. For the fluorescence determination, the recoveries range from 95.1 % to 104.8 %, and the relative standard deviations (RSD) range from 1.00 % to 3.22 %. For the colorimetric method, the recoveries are in the range of 97.6 %-104.0 %, and RSD are in the range of 0.46 %-2.38 %. The above results confirmed that the proposed dual-mode system was applicable for CP assay in pharmaceutical samples with good precision and selectivity.

In our study, the dual-mode sensor for CP was successfully developed using fluorescent and colorimetric assays based on oxTMB and O-CDs. By monitoring fluorescence or absorption change of the sensing system, impressive analytical performances for CP sensing including wide linear range and lower detection limits were obtained. In addition, the established sensor had been successfully applied to monitor CP in pharmaceutical formulations, indicating its considerable potential for future applications. Moreover, the dual-mode assays have virtues of fast response and easy operation. What’s more, the mutual corroboration of colorimetric and fluorescence results guaranteed the accuracy of this sensing system.

Maolin Wu: Conceptualization, Validation, Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Yuntai Lv: Data curation, Formal analysis. Zihan Lin: Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121683.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chitosan-stabilized platinum nanoparticles as effective oxidase mimics for colorimetric detection of acid phosphatase
Nanoscale

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.H. Deng, X.L. Lin, Y.H. Liu, K.L. Li, Q.Q. Zhuang, H.P. Peng, A.L. Liu, X.H. Xia, W. Chen, Chitosan-stabilized platinum nanoparticles as effective oxidase mimics for colorimetric detection of acid phosphatase, Nanoscale 9(29) (2017) 10292-10300.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent Advances in Nanozymes: From Matters to Bioapplications
Adv. Funct. Mater.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Perspectives for Single-Atom Nanozymes: Advanced Synthesis, Functional Mechanisms, and Biomedical Applications
